case_title: Judgment Sheet IN THE PESHAWAR HIGH COURT
other_citation: Page 3: The Court is mindful that hostility does not always reveal itself at the commencement of testimony.
summary: The petitioner sought permission to cross-examine a witness who had deviated from his earlier statement. The Court deliberates on whether such conduct justifies designating the witness as hostile, considering historical and modern legal contexts.
case: Cr. Rev No. 120-P of 2026 Jalal Ahmad Vs Bilal etc
remarks:
i. A concession extracted through legitimate forensic skill cannot, by itself, justify a declaration of hostility. Were the law otherwise, every successful cross-examination would become a ground for invoking Article 150. The Court must therefore inquire whether the answer given represents a truthful acknowledgment elicited through proper questioning or whether it constitutes a conscious and unexplained departure from an earlier position.
ii. The power to permit the cross-examination of one\'s own witness is undoubtedly discretionary, but that discretion must be exercised upon objective considerations. The Court should ordinarily examine: (i) whether the witness has materially departed from a previous statement on a matter of significance; (ii) whether the departure appears deliberate, evasive, or calculated to assist one side of the litigation; (iii) whether the altered version has caused genuine surprise to the party calling the witness; (iv) whether the inconsistency is capable of innocent explanation; and (v) whether the interests of justice require further testing of the witness through questions ordinarily permissible in cross-examination.
iii. the doctrine of hostile witnesses is not a doctrine about procedural stages but a doctrine about the reliability of testimony. Hostility is determined not by the point at which it manifests itself but by the conduct through which it is revealed. A witness is not hostile because he weakens a case; he becomes hostile when he abandons the obligation of truth.
iv. Had the legislature intended to confine the power to examination-in-chief alone, it could easily have done so in express terms. Instead, the statutory emphasis is placed upon the satisfaction of the Court and not upon the stage of the proceedings. The interpretation that the Court is inclined to adopt also finds substantial support from Indian jurisprudence, which has examined the doctrine with considerable depth.
v. The jurisdiction to invoke Article 150 arises not because a particular stage of examination has been reached, but because the conduct of the witness has disclosed circumstances rendering the ordinary testing of credibility inadequate. Once such circumstances appear, whether during examination-in-chief, cross-examination, re-examination, or at any other point before the witness is discharged, the Court remains empowered to act. Any contrary interpretation would elevate procedural chronology above the administration of justice and would frustrate the very purpose for which the doctrine of hostile witnesses evolved.
vi. If a witness were permitted to support one version at one stage of the proceedings and thereafter consciously depart from it on a material aspect without any apprehension of legal consequence, the result would be to undermine public confidence in the judicial process itself.
vii. The declaration of hostility is not a substitute for accountability. It is merely an evidentiary mechanism designed to permit further testing of credibility. Where circumstances disclose that a witness has knowingly departed from the truth, willfully suppressed material facts, or consciously made statements inconsistent with what he knows to be true, the matter may travel beyond the confines of Article 150 and enter the domain of offences relating to false evidence.
viii. The consistent and principled application of those provisions serves an important public purpose. It discourages deliberate falsehood, reinforces respect for the oath administered by the Court, and ensures that witnesses do not proceed upon the mistaken belief that calculated departures from the truth are devoid of legal consequence.
